title: Your Apology for Origen did not save him but involved you in heresy.

source.title: NPNF2-03. Theodoret, Jerome, Gennadius, & Rufinus: Historical Writings

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But what defence can you make in reference to the Apology which you have written for the works of Origen, or rather in reference to the book of Eusebius, though you have altered much, and translated the work of a heretic under the title of a martyr, yet you have set down still more which is incompatible with the faith of the church.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You as well as I turn Latin books into Greek; can you prohibit me from giving the works of a foreigner to my own people?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If I had made my answer in the case of some other work of yours in which you had not attacked me, it might have been thought that, in translating what you had already translated, I was acting in hostility to you, and wishing to prove you inaccurate or untrustworthy.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But this is a new kind of complaint, when you take it amiss that an answer is made you on a point on which you have accused me.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: All Rome was said to have been upset by your translation; every one was demanding of me a remedy for this; not that I was of any account, but that those who asked this thought me so.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You say that you who had made the translation were my friend.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But what would you have had me do?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Ought we to obey God or man?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: To guard our master’s property or to conceal the theft of a fellow-servant?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Can I not be at peace with you unless I join with you in committing acts which bring reproach?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If you had not mentioned my name, if you had not tricked me out in your flatteries, I might have had some way of escape, and have made many excuses for not translating what had already been translated.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But you, my friend, have compelled me to waste a good many days on this work, and to bring out before the public eye what should have been engulfed in Charybdis; yet still, though I had been injured, I observed the laws of friendship, and as far as possible defended myself without accusing you.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It is a too suspicious and complaining temper which you shew when you take home to yourself as a reproach what was spoken against the heretics.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If it is impossible to be your friend unless I am the friend of heretics, I shall more easily put up with your enmity than with their friendship.
